title: Materialized view
facts: In any database management system following the relational model, a view is a virtual table representing the result of a database query.
Whenever a query or an update addresses an ordinary view's virtual table, the DBMS converts these into queries or updates against the underlying base tables.
A materialized view takes a different approach: the query result is cached as a concrete ("materialized") table.
A materialized view may be updated from the original base tables from time to time.
A materialized view enables much more efficient access.
A materialized view has the cost of extra storage.
A materialized view has the cost of some data being potentially out-of-date.
Materialized views find use especially in data warehousing scenarios.
In data warehousing scenarios, frequent queries of the actual base tables can be expensive.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the definition of views, the behavior of ordinary views, and the characteristics, costs, and use cases of materialized views. No major claims are missing, and the fact set would preserve answers to factual questions from the passage.

"A materialized view takes a different approach: the query result is cached as a concrete (\"materialized\") table.",
"A materialized view may be updated from the original base tables from time to time.",
"A materialized view enables much more efficient access.",
"A materialized view has the cost of extra storage.",
"A materialized view has the cost of some data being potentially out-of-date.",
"Materialized views find use especially in data warehousing scenarios.",
"In data warehousing scenarios, frequent queries of the actual base tables can be expensive."
]
}

title: Materialized view
facts: Microsoft SQL Server implements materialized views via a concept known as 'Indexed Views'.
Microsoft SQL Server differs from other RDBMS in its implementation of materialized views.
Indexed views do not require a refresh.
Indexed views are always synchronized to the original data of the tables that compound the view.
For indexed views, the mapping between origin and destination lines must be deterministic.
The deterministic mapping requirement limits the types of possible queries for indexed views.
The indexed view mechanism has been available since SQL Server 2000.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage. The passage explicitly states that SQL Server uses 'Indexed Views' for materialized views, differs from other RDBMS, that indexed views do not require refresh and are always synchronized, that the mapping must be deterministic which limits query types, and that this mechanism has been available since SQL Server 2000.

"Indexed views do not require a refresh.",
"Indexed views are always synchronized to the original data of the tables that compound the view.",
"For indexed views, the mapping between origin and destination lines must be deterministic.",
"The deterministic mapping requirement limits the types of possible queries for indexed views.",
"The indexed view mechanism has been available since SQL Server 2000."
]
}

title: Inverted index
facts: An inverted index is also referred to as a postings list, postings file, or inverted file.
An inverted index is a database index storing a mapping from content to its locations in a table, document, or set of documents.
An inverted index is named in contrast to a forward index, which maps from documents to content.
The purpose of an inverted index is to allow fast full-text searches.
The purpose of an inverted index comes at a cost of increased processing when a document is added to the database.
The inverted file may be the database file itself, rather than its index.
The inverted index is the most popular data structure used in document retrieval systems.
The inverted index is used on a large scale in search engines.
Several significant general-purpose mainframe-based database management systems have used inverted list architectures.
Those systems include ADABAS, DATACOM/DB, and Model 204.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly entailed by the passage. The fact set covers the definition, alternative names, contrast with forward index, purpose, cost, inverted file as database file, popularity, use in search engines, and specific database systems. No major claims from the passage are omitted, so the fact set would preserve answers to ordinary factual questions.

title: Inverted index
facts: There are two main variants of inverted indexes.
A record-level inverted index contains a list of references to documents for each word.
A record-level inverted index is also called an inverted file index or just inverted file.
A word-level inverted index contains the positions of each word within a document.
A word-level inverted index is also called a full inverted index or inverted list.
The word-level inverted index offers more functionality like phrase searches.
The word-level inverted index needs more processing power and space to be created.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the two variants, their names, and the additional features of the word-level index. No claims are missing or unsupported.

title: Inverted index
facts: The inverted index data structure is a central component of a typical search engine indexing algorithm.
A goal of a search engine implementation is to optimize the speed of the query.
The query finds the documents where word X occurs.
A forward index stores lists of words per document.
A forward index is inverted to develop an inverted index.
Querying the forward index requires sequential iteration through each document and to each word to verify a matching document.
The time, memory, and processing resources to perform a query on the forward index are not always technically realistic.
The inverted index data structure lists the documents per word.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the inverted index, forward index, query optimization, and the inversion process.

title: Inverted index
facts: Inverted indexes are important in sequence assembly of short fragments of sequenced DNA.
One way to find the source of a fragment is to search it against a reference DNA sequence.
A small number of mismatches can be accounted for by dividing the fragment into smaller fragments.
At least one subfragment is likely to match the reference DNA sequence.
The matching requires constructing an inverted index of all substrings of a certain length from the reference DNA sequence.
Human DNA contains more than 3 billion base pairs.
The inverted index stores a DNA substring for every index and a 32-bit integer for the index itself.
The storage requirement for such an inverted index would probably be in the tens of gigabytes.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the key points about inverted indexes in DNA sequence assembly, the method for handling mismatches, the construction of the index, and storage requirements. No major claims are missing, and the fact set would preserve answers to factual questions from the passage.

title: Vector space model
facts: As documents are added to the document collection, the region defined by the hypercube's vertices becomes more populated.
As documents are added to the document collection, the region defined by the hypercube's vertices becomes denser.
Unlike Boolean model, when a document is added using term frequency-inverse document frequency weights, the inverse document frequencies of the terms in the new document decrease.
Unlike Boolean model, when a document is added using term frequency-inverse document frequency weights, the inverse document frequencies of the remaining terms increase.
On average, as documents are added, the region where documents lie expands.
On average, as documents are added, the expansion of the region regulates the density of the entire collection representation.
The behavior models the original motivation of Salton and his colleagues that a document collection represented in a low density region could yield better retrieval results.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage. The passage explicitly states that the hypercube region becomes more populated and denser, that IDF decreases for new document terms and increases for remaining terms unlike Boolean, that the region expands on average regulating density, and that this behavior models Salton's motivation for low density yielding better retrieval.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Latent semantic analysis (LSA) is a technique in natural language processing.
LSA is a technique in distributional semantics.
LSA analyzes relationships between a set of documents and the terms they contain.
LSA produces a set of concepts related to the documents and terms.
LSA assumes that words that are close in meaning will occur in similar pieces of text.
This assumption is the distributional hypothesis.
A matrix containing word counts per document is constructed from a large piece of text.
In the matrix, rows represent unique words and columns represent each document.
Singular value decomposition (SVD) is used to reduce the number of rows.
SVD preserves the similarity structure among columns.
Documents are compared by cosine similarity between any two columns.
Values close to 1 represent very similar documents.
Values close to 0 represent very dissimilar documents.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the definition, assumption, matrix construction, SVD, cosine similarity, and interpretation. No major claims are missing, and the fact set preserves answers to factual questions from the passage.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: LSA can use a document-term matrix.
The document-term matrix describes occurrences of terms in documents.
The document-term matrix is a sparse matrix.
Rows of the document-term matrix correspond to terms.
Columns of the document-term matrix correspond to documents.
A typical example of weighting the elements of the matrix is tf-idf.
tf-idf stands for term frequency–inverse document frequency.
The weight of an element of the matrix is proportional to the number of times the terms appear in each document.
Rare terms are upweighted to reflect their relative importance.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the document-term matrix, its structure, and tf-idf weighting. No unsupported facts or missing major claims.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Rank lowering merges dimensions associated with terms that have similar meanings.
Rank lowering mitigates the problem of identifying synonymy.
Rank lowering partially mitigates the problem with polysemy.
Components of polysemous words that point in the 'right' direction are added to components of words that share a similar meaning.
Components of polysemous words that point in other directions tend to either cancel out or be smaller than components in directions corresponding to the intended sense.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Latent Semantic Analysis has been used in the study of human memory.
Latent Semantic Analysis has been used in areas of free recall and memory search.

There is a positive correlation between the semantic similarity of two words as measured by LSA and the probability that the words would be recalled one after another in free recall tasks using study lists of random common nouns.
The inter-response time between similar words was much quicker than between dissimilar words in these situations.
These findings are referred to as the Semantic Proximity Effect.

judge.rationale: All five facts are directly stated in the passage, covering the use of LSA in memory, free recall, memory search, the positive correlation, quicker inter-response times, and the Semantic Proximity Effect.

}

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Latent semantic analysis results can be justified on the mathematical level.
Latent semantic analysis results have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language.
A component (1.3452 * car + 0.2828 * bottle) could be justified.
Bottles and cars have transparent and opaque parts.
Bottles and cars are man made.
Bottles and cars have high probability of containing logos/words on their surface.
Bottles and cars share semantics in many ways.
Within a language in question, there may not be a readily available word to assign.
Explainability becomes an analysis task as opposed to simple word/class/concept assignment task.

We are given a passage and a list of generated facts. We need to evaluate each fact for entailment from the passage. The passage is:

"will occur. This leads to results which can be justified on the mathematical level, but have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language. Though, the (1.3452 * car + 0.2828 * bottle) component could be justified because both bottles and cars have transparent and opaque parts, are man made and with high probability contain logos/words on their surface; thus, in many ways these two concepts "share semantics." That is, within a language in question, there may not be a readily available word to assign and explainability becomes an analysis task as opposed to simple word/class/concept assignment task."

Note: The passage starts with "will occur." which seems like a fragment, but we take it as given.

Now evaluate each fact:

1. "Latent semantic analysis results can be justified on the mathematical level." The passage says "This leads to results which can be justified on the mathematical level". The "This" refers to something before, but the passage mentions "results" that can be justified mathematically. The fact says "Latent semantic analysis results" - the passage does not explicitly mention "latent semantic analysis", but it is implied by the context? The passage talks about a component (1.3452 * car + 0.2828 * bottle) which is typical of latent semantic analysis (LSA). However, the passage does not explicitly say "latent semantic analysis". The fact introduces a term not in the passage. But the passage says "results which can be justified on the mathematical level". The fact says "Latent semantic analysis results can be justified on the mathematical level." This is a reasonable inference but the passage does not mention LSA by name. However, the fact is about "results" and the passage says "results which can be justified on the mathematical level". So the core claim is entailed: results can be justified mathematically. The addition of "latent semantic analysis" might be considered a specification that is not directly stated. But the passage is clearly about LSA-like results. I think it's entailed because the passage is describing LSA results. But to be strict, the passage does not name LSA. However, the fact is essentially saying the same thing. I'll consider it entailed.

2. "Latent semantic analysis results have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language." The passage says "but have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language." Again, it says "results" without specifying LSA. But the fact adds "latent semantic analysis". Same reasoning: the core claim is entailed.

3. "A component (1.3452 * car + 0.2828 * bottle) could be justified." The passage says "Though, the (1.3452 * car + 0.2828 * bottle) component could be justified". This is directly stated. Entailed.

4. "Bottles and cars have transparent and opaque parts." The passage says "because both bottles and cars have transparent and opaque parts". Directly stated. Entailed.

5. "Bottles and cars are man made." The passage says "are man made". Directly stated. Entailed.

6. "Bottles and cars have high probability of containing logos/words on their surface." The passage says "with high probability contain logos/words on their surface". Directly stated. Entailed.

7. "Bottles and cars share semantics in many ways." The passage says "in many ways these two concepts 'share semantics.'" Directly stated. Entailed.

8. "Within a language in question, there may not be a readily available word to assign." The passage says "within a language in question, there may not be a readily available word to assign". Directly stated. Entailed.

9. "Explainability becomes an analysis task as opposed to simple word/class/concept assignment task." The passage says "explainability becomes an analysis task as opposed to simple word/class/concept assignment task." Directly stated. Entailed.

So all facts seem entailed. But note: facts 1 and 2 add "latent semantic analysis" which is not explicitly in the passage. However, the passage is clearly about that context. The instruction says "Use only the passage as ground truth." So we should only consider what is directly stated. The passage does not mention "latent semantic analysis". It says "results" and "the component". So facts 1 and 2 are not directly entailed because they add a term not present. But the core claim is the same. The instruction says "A fact is entailed if the passage directly supports it." Does the passage directly support that "latent semantic analysis results" have those properties? The passage says "results" which could be interpreted as LSA results, but it's not explicit. To be safe, I should mark them as unsupported because the passage does not mention LSA. However, the passage is clearly about LSA, but we must be strict. Let's see the context: The passage starts with "will occur." which is a fragment. It might be from a larger text about LSA. But we only have this passage. The fact says "Latent semantic analysis results" - the passage does not contain that phrase. So I think facts 1 and 2 are not directly entailed. They are inferences. So I will mark them as unsupported.

But wait: The passage says "This leads to results which can be justified on the mathematical level". The "This" refers to something, but we don't know what. It could be LSA. But the fact explicitly names LSA. Since the passage does not name LSA, the fact is not directly supported. So I'll mark fact 1 and 2 as unsupported.

Now, missing major claims: The passage has a main claim about explainability becoming an analysis task. That is covered in fact 9. Also the idea that there may not be a readily available word is covered. The component justification is covered. So no major missing claims.

QA fidelity pass: Would the set of facts preserve answers to ordinary factual questions answerable from the passage? If we remove facts 1 and 2, we still have the core information about the component, the properties of bottles and cars, and the explainability task. But the passage also says "results which can be justified on the mathematical level, but have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language." That is not captured by any of the other facts if we remove 1 and 2. So a question like "What can be said about the results?" would not be answerable from the remaining facts. So the fact set would miss that claim. Therefore, qa_fidelity_pass should be false because the fact set does not include that major claim. But we have fact 1 and 2 which do capture it, but they are unsupported. So if we consider only entailed facts, the set is missing that claim. The instruction says "The QA fidelity pass should be true only if the fact set would preserve answers to ordinary factual questions answerable from the passage." The fact set includes all generated facts, but we are to judge based on the fact set as given. The fact set includes facts 1 and 2, even if they are unsupported. But the question is about the fact set preserving answers. If the fact set includes unsupported facts, that might still preserve answers, but they are not faithful to the passage. The instruction says "You judge source fidelity for fact rephrasing." So we are evaluating the fact set's fidelity. The qa_fidelity_pass is about whether the fact set (as a whole) would preserve answers to questions from the passage. If the fact set includes unsupported facts, it might still preserve answers, but it might also introduce inaccuracies. However, the fact set includes facts 1 and 2 which are essentially correct but not directly supported. They do capture the claims from the passage. So if we consider the fact set as is, it does preserve the answers. But the instruction says "Use only the passage as ground truth." So we should only consider facts that are entailed. The qa_fidelity_pass should be based on the entailed facts? The description: "The QA fidelity pass should be true only if the fact set would preserve answers to ordinary factual questions answerable from the passage." It doesn't say "only entailed facts". It says "the fact set". So we consider the entire generated fact set. Since facts 1 and 2 are in the set, and they correctly capture the passage's claims (even if not directly entailed due to naming), the fact set would preserve answers. But wait, the fact set also includes unsupported facts? Actually, facts 1 and 2 are unsupported because they add "latent semantic analysis". But the passage does talk about results that can be justified mathematically and have no obvious meaning. So the fact set does contain that information. So a question like "What can be said about the results?" would be answered by fact 1 or 2. So the fact set preserves that answer. However, the fact set might also contain inaccuracies? No, the facts are accurate to the passage's content. The only issue is the addition of "latent semantic analysis" which is not in the passage but is a reasonable inference. But the instruction says "Use only the passage as ground truth." So if we strictly adhere, the fact set includes claims that are not directly from the passage. But the qa_fidelity_pass is about preserving answers, not about strict entailment. So if the fact set includes those claims, it does preserve answers. But the fact set also includes all other entailed facts. So I think qa_fidelity_pass should be true because the fact set covers all major claims from the passage. The missing major claims? I said there are no missing major claims because facts 1 and 2 cover the results claims. But if we consider facts 1 and 2 as unsupported, then the entailed fact set would miss those claims. But the qa_fidelity_pass is about the entire fact set, not just entailed ones. So I'll set qa_fidelity_pass to true.

But let's double-check: The passage says "This leads to results which can be justified on the mathematical level, but have no immediately obvious meaning in natural language." That is a major claim. The fact set includes fact 1 and 2 which capture that. So yes, the fact set preserves that answer

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: LSA can only partially capture polysemy.
LSA treats each occurrence of a word as having the same meaning.
LSA represents a word as a single point in space.
LSA considers the occurrence of 'chair' in 'The Chair of the Board' and in 'the chair maker' as the same.
The vector representation in LSA is an average of all the word's different meanings in the corpus.
The averaging of meanings can make comparison difficult.
The effect of averaging is often lessened due to words having a predominant sense throughout a corpus.
Not all meanings of a word are equally likely in a corpus.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly entailed by the passage. The passage explicitly states that LSA partially captures polysemy, treats each word occurrence as having the same meaning due to a single point representation, gives the 'chair' example, explains the vector is an average of meanings, notes that averaging can hinder comparison, and mentions the effect is lessened by predominant senses where not all meanings are equally likely. No claims are unsupported, and no major claims from the passage are missing.

]

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: In semantic hashing documents are mapped to memory addresses by means of a neural network.
In semantic hashing semantically similar documents are located at nearby addresses.
A deep neural network essentially builds a graphical model of the word-count vectors obtained from a large set of documents.
Documents similar to a query document can be found by simply accessing all the addresses that differ by only a few bits from the address of the query document.
The way of extending the efficiency of hash-coding to approximate matching is closely related to locality sensitive hashing.

judge.rationale: All five generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the mapping to addresses, nearby locations for similar documents, the graphical model of word-count vectors, the method for finding similar documents, and the relation to locality sensitive hashing. No claims are unsupported or missing.

No claims are unsupported or missing."

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Latent semantic indexing (LSI) is an indexing and retrieval method.
LSI uses a mathematical technique called singular value decomposition (SVD).
LSI identifies patterns in the relationships between the terms and concepts contained in an unstructured collection of text.
LSI is based on the principle that words that are used in the same contexts tend to have similar meanings.
A key feature of LSI is its ability to extract the conceptual content of a body of text.
LSI establishes associations between terms that occur in similar contexts.

judge.rationale: All six generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the definition, technique, pattern identification, principle, key feature, and association establishment. No unsupported facts or missing major claims.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Latent semantic indexing is called latent semantic indexing because of its ability to correlate semantically related terms that are latent in a collection of text.
Latent semantic indexing was first applied to text at Bellcore in the late 1980s.
Latent semantic indexing is also called latent semantic analysis.
Latent semantic indexing uncovers the underlying latent semantic structure in the usage of words in a body of text.
Latent semantic indexing can be used to extract the meaning of the text in response to user queries.
User queries are commonly referred to as concept searches.
Queries against a set of documents that have undergone LSI return results that are conceptually similar in meaning to the search criteria.
The results from LSI queries may not share a specific word or words with the search criteria.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the naming, origin, alternative name, functionality, and query behavior of latent semantic indexing. No claims are unsupported or missing.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: LSI is used to perform automated document categorization.
Experiments have demonstrated correlations between the way LSI and humans process and categorize text.
Document categorization is the assignment of documents to one or more predefined categories based on the similarity of the documents to the conceptual content of the categories.
LSI uses example documents to establish the conceptual basis for each category.
During categorization processing, the concepts contained in the documents being categorized are compared to the concepts contained in the example items.
A category or categories is assigned to the documents based on the similarities between the concepts contained in the documents and the concepts contained in the example documents.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: LSI uses a strictly mathematical approach.
LSI is inherently independent of language because it uses a strictly mathematical approach.
LSI's language independence enables it to elicit semantic content of information in any language without requiring auxiliary structures such as dictionaries and thesauri.
LSI can perform cross-linguistic concept searching.
LSI can perform example-based categorization.
Queries can be made in one language, such as English.
Conceptually similar results are returned even if they are composed of an entirely different language or of multiple languages.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: A is the supplied m by n weighted matrix of term frequencies in a collection of text.
m is the number of unique terms.
n is the number of documents.
T is a computed m by r matrix of term vectors.
r is the rank of A.
r is a measure of A's unique dimensions.
r is ≤ min(m,n).
S is a computed r by r diagonal matrix of decreasing singular values.
D is a computed n by r matrix of document vectors.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering all major claims about A, m, n, T, r, S, and D.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: The same steps are used to locate the vectors representing the text of queries and new documents within the document space of an existing LSI index.
By a simple transformation of the A = T S D^T equation into the equivalent D = A^T T S^{-1} equation, a new vector d for a query or for a new document can be created.
The new vector d is created by computing a new column in A and then multiplying the new column by T S^{-1}.
The new column in A is computed using the originally derived global term weights.
The new column in A is computed by applying the same local weighting function to the terms in the query or in the new document.

judge.rationale: All facts are directly supported by the passage. Fact 0 is a direct quote. Facts 1 and 2 match the transformation and creation steps. Facts 3 and 4 correctly state the components used to compute the new column. No major claims are missing, and the facts preserve answers to factual questions from the passage.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: A drawback to computing vectors in this way when adding new searchable documents is that terms unknown during the SVD phase for the original index are ignored.
Terms unknown during the SVD phase for the original index have no impact on the global weights and learned correlations derived from the original collection of text.
The computed vectors for the new text are still very relevant for similarity comparisons with all other document vectors.

judge.rationale: All three facts are directly supported by the passage: fact 1 restates the drawback, fact 2 restates the impact on weights and correlations, and fact 3 restates the relevance for similarity comparisons. title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Folding in is the process of augmenting the document vector spaces for an LSI index with new documents.
The folding-in process does not account for the new semantic content of the new text.
Adding a substantial number of documents through folding-in provides good results for queries as long as the terms and concepts they contain are well represented within the LSI index.
When the terms and concepts of a new set of documents need to be included in an LSI index, either the term-document matrix and the SVD must be recomputed.
When the terms and concepts of a new set of documents need to be included in an LSI index, an incremental update method is needed.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage. The facts cover the definition of folding in, its limitation regarding new semantic content, the condition for good query results, and the two options for including new terms/concepts. No major claims are missing, and the entailed facts preserve answers to factual questions from the passage.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Early challenges to LSI focused on scalability and performance.
LSI requires relatively high computational performance and memory in comparison to other information retrieval techniques.
The high computational performance and memory requirements of LSI have been largely overcome with modern high-speed processors and inexpensive memory.
Real-world applications involving more than 30 million documents that were fully processed through the matrix and SVD computations are common in some LSI applications.
A fully scalable implementation of LSI is contained in the open source gensim software package.
The gensim implementation supports an unlimited number of documents and online training.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering early challenges, requirements, overcoming them, real-world applications, and the gensim implementation details.

title: Latent semantic analysis
facts: Another challenge to LSI is the alleged difficulty in determining the optimal number of dimensions to use for performing the SVD.
Fewer dimensions allow for broader comparisons of the concepts contained in a collection of text.
A higher number of dimensions enable more specific (or more relevant) comparisons of concepts.
The actual number of dimensions that can be used is limited by the number of documents in the collection.
Research has demonstrated that around 300 dimensions will usually provide the best results with moderate-sized document collections (hundreds of thousands of documents).
Research has demonstrated that perhaps 400 dimensions for larger document collections (millions of documents).
Recent studies indicate that 50-1000 dimensions are suitable depending on the size and nature of the document collection.
Checking the proportion of variance retained, similar to PCA or factor analysis, to determine the optimal dimensionality is not suitable for LSI.
Using a synonym test is one possible method to find the correct dimensionality.
Prediction of missing words is another possible method to find the correct dimensionality.
When LSI topics are used as features in supervised learning methods, one can use prediction error measurements to find the ideal dimensionality.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage. No unsupported facts or missing major claims. The set of entailed facts covers all key information from the passage, so QA fidelity is preserved.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a technique.
RAG enables large language models (LLMs) to retrieve and incorporate new information from external data sources.
With RAG, LLMs first refer to a specified set of documents and then respond to user queries.
The documents supplement information from the LLM's pre-existing training data.
RAG allows LLMs to use domain-specific and/or updated information that is not available in the training data.
RAG enables LLM-based chatbots to access internal company data.
RAG enables LLM-based chatbots to generate responses based on authoritative sources.
RAG was first proposed in 2020.
RAG has since become a widely adopted approach in modern AI systems.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly supported by the passage, covering the definition, process, benefits, examples, and historical context of RAG. No claims are unsupported, and no major claims from the passage are missing.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: RAG improves LLMs by incorporating information retrieval before generating responses.
LLMs rely on static training data.
RAG pulls relevant text from databases, uploaded documents, or web sources.
According to Ars Technica, RAG is a way of improving LLM performance by blending the LLM process with a web search or other document look-up process to help LLMs stick to the facts.
RAG helps reduce AI hallucinations.
AI hallucinations have caused chatbots to describe policies that do not exist.
AI hallucinations have caused chatbots to recommend nonexistent legal cases to lawyers looking for citations.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: LLMs can provide incorrect information.
Google first demonstrated its LLM tool 'Google Bard'.
Google Bard was later re-branded to Gemini.
The LLM provided incorrect information about the James Webb Space Telescope.
The error contributed to a $100 billion decline in Google's stock value.
RAG is used to prevent these errors.
RAG does not solve all the problems.
LLMs can generate misinformation even when pulling from factually correct sources if they misinterpret the context.
MIT Technology Review gives an example of an AI-generated response stating, 'The United States has had one Muslim president, Barack Hussein Obama.'

The model retrieved this from the rhetorical chapter title 'Barack Hussein Obama: America's First Muslim President?' in the book Faith in the New Millennium: The Future of Religion and American Politics.
The LLM did not 'know' or 'understand' the context of the title.
The LLM generated a false statement.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances large language models (LLMs).
RAG incorporates an information-retrieval mechanism.
The information-retrieval mechanism allows models to access and utilize additional data beyond the models' original training set.

Ars Technica notes that when new information becomes available, rather than having to retrain the model, all that is needed is to augment the model's external knowledge base with the updated information.
IBM states that in the generative phase, the LLM draws from the augmented prompt and the LLM's internal representation of the LLM's training data to synthesize an answer.

judge.rationale: All five facts are directly supported by the passage. Fact 0 matches the first sentence. Fact 1 matches 'by incorporating an information-retrieval mechanism'. Fact 2 matches 'allows models to access and utilize additional data beyond their original training set'. Fact 3 is a direct quote from Ars Technica. Fact 4 is a direct quote from IBM with minor pronoun adjustment. No unsupported facts or missing major claims; the facts cover the key points of the passage.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: Retro redesigns the language model with the retriever in mind.
A 25-time smaller network using Retro gets comparable perplexity as much larger language models.
Retro is trained from scratch.
Retro incurs the high cost of training runs that the original RAG scheme avoided.
The hypothesis is that by giving domain knowledge during training, Retro needs less focus on the domain.
The hypothesis is that Retro can devote Retro's smaller weight resources only to language semantics.
The redesigned language model is shown.

title: Retrieval-augmented generation
facts: RAG improves the accuracy of large language models (LLMs).
RAG does not eliminate all challenges.
RAG reduces the need for frequent model retraining.
RAG does not remove the need for model retraining entirely.
LLMs may struggle to recognize when they lack sufficient information to provide a reliable response.
Without specific training, models may generate answers even when they should indicate uncertainty.
According to IBM, the issue of models generating answers when they should indicate uncertainty can arise when the model lacks the ability to assess its own knowledge limitations.

judge.rationale: All generated facts are directly entailed by the passage. No major claims from the passage are omitted, and the fact set would preserve answers to ordinary factual questions.
